slides.description: This is the inaugural slide, titled "AI Screenplays" in a bold blue font. The slide features a cartoon illustration, reminiscent of the style from South Park, depicting an office scene. A character in a suit stands behind a large wooden desk, which is adorned with two Oscar-like statues, an Emmy-like award, and a red trophy labeled 'CARTMAN'. Behind him, framed by a window overlooking the Hollywood sign and mountains at sunset, is a cardboard box robot named "A.W.E.S.O.M.-O." On the far right, a framed picture on the wall prominently displays "AWESOME-O" with a red 'LAME' stamp across it. The desk also holds stacks of papers that appear to be 'SCRIPTS', along with a framed family photo and an aquarium containing an alien figurine. This slide effectively introduces the topic of AI in screenplay writing, using a humorous and recognizable pop culture reference to frame the discussion.
slides.narration: Okay, so, yo, everyone, I'm your instructor for this awesome journey, and like, check out our opening slide, right? Today, dude, we're diving deep into 'AI Screenplays,' because, you know, wouldn't it be cool if AI could actually write an entire, super coherent movie script? The thing is, generating really long, consistent documents with AI, it's actually a huge challenge—we often see 'context drift' and weird stuff happening, like, the AI gets lost. But what we're gonna explore is this super powerful 'agentic flow' architecture that totally breaks down these complex tasks. It ensures our AI can build a story, step by step, ensuring something truly cohesive from start to finish. It's gonna be pretty mind-blowing, I think.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Generating Long Documents," shifts from the humorous introduction of AI in screenplays to a more concrete outline of the lecture's scope. It explains that AI can generate long, coherent text documents, listing examples such as books, reports, and, crucially, screenplays, directly connecting back to the previous slide's theme. The slide then details the immediate goal: to build an "agentic flow" specifically for generating movie screenplays. Finally, it notes that this architectural approach for generating long texts can be generalized beyond screenplays to other document types, suggesting broader applicability while maintaining the focus on the initial topic.
slides.narration: So, yeah, I mean, it *can* be mind-blowing because AI *can* totally generate long, coherent text documents, right? Like, we're talking full books, detailed reports, and of course, what we're focused on today, screenplays. So, our main goal, like, for this lecture, is we're gonna build out this whole 'agentic flow' specifically for generating movie screenplays. But just a heads up, dude, this flow architecture, it's super powerful, and you can totally generalize it to all sorts of other long text documents. So, yeah, it's not just for movies, you know?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form," pivots from the previous slide's objective to detail the core difficulty in generating extensive documents like screenplays. It explicitly states that it is challenging for an AI to produce a long, coherent document in a single, one-shot attempt. The slide elaborates on this challenge, highlighting the "Local vs global" problem where AI can write small, localized parts well but fails to maintain overarching coherence across the entire document. This often results in the AI becoming "lost in the middle" or experiencing "context drift," causing it to hallucinate or lose focus deep into the generation process. Thus, this slide lays out the fundamental problem that the "agentic flow" approach, introduced in Slide 2 for generating movie screenplays, is designed to overcome.
slides.narration: Okay, so yeah, AI *can* totally generate long documents, right? But here's the *big* challenge, bro: it's super difficult for an AI to generate a *long*, coherent document in just one shot. Like, the AI might be really good at writing small parts, like a single paragraph or a scene, but it struggles, man, to maintain that overall coherence across the *entire* document. It becomes, you know, "lost in the middle." We call it "context drift," where deep into the document, the AI can start to, like, hallucinate or totally lose focus of what it's even supposed to be writing about. It's a real problem.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Movie Screenplays," elaborates on the complexity inherent in generating a screenplay, directly addressing the challenge of one-shot long-form generation introduced in Slide 3. It first highlights the many different elements an AI needs to capture, such as story shape, the goals of sequences, and scene action and dialogue. The slide then details the hierarchical structure of a screenplay, breaking it down into premise, story arc, sequences, and scenes, moving from basic elements to increasingly detailed plot points. By outlining these multiple layers and elements, the slide implicitly explains why a simple "one-shot" AI generation struggles to maintain coherence and structure across an entire movie screenplay, further setting the stage for the "agentic flow" solution proposed in Slide 2.
slides.narration: So, like, why is that one-shot challenge so hard, especially for something like a movie screenplay, right? Well, dude, screenplays are actually super complex! There are just so many different elements an AI needs to capture simultaneously. We're talking about the overall story shape, the goals of each narrative 'sequence,' and then, like, the really granular scene action and dialogue. And then, there's this whole hierarchical structure to a screenplay, you know? It goes from a basic premise, to a high-level story arc, then breaks into more detailed sequences, and finally, even more detailed scenes. It's this multi-layered complexity that totally makes it impossible for an AI to just spit out a perfect, coherent screenplay in one go, you know?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Screenplay Flow," visually illustrates the multi-step, hierarchical process proposed for generating screenplays, directly building upon the concepts from previous slides. It presents a flowchart diagram, showing four distinct stages: "Premise," "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes," each with a corresponding JSON file indicated (e.g., "premise.json"). Arrows indicate a progression from Premise to Arc, then from Arc to Sequences and Scenes, demonstrating how the generative process breaks down the complex task into manageable, sequential steps. This visual flow provides a concrete representation of the "agentic flow" introduced in Slide 2, addressing the "one-shot" challenge from Slide 3 and elaborating on the hierarchical screenplay structure detailed in Slide 4 by showing how these elements are processed in a structured manner.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've seen how complex screenplays are, right, and why one-shot generation totally doesn't work, this slide, 'Screenplay Flow,' visually shows you our 'agentic flow' architecture. It literally breaks down that massive, impossible task into these manageable, sequential steps. So, you start with the 'Premise,' which feeds into the 'Arc,' and then from that Arc, we generate 'Sequences' and then finally, 'Scenes.' Each of these steps, you know, generates a structured JSON file, like 'premise.json' or 'arc.json.' This ensures we build context layer by layer, dude, totally preventing that 'context drift' we talked about.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Premise Agent," elaborates on the initial step of the multi-stage screenplay generation process introduced in Slide 5, specifically detailing the 'Premise' stage. It describes the Premise Agent as a chat agent that interactively discusses the core elements of a movie, including its title, logline, characters, genre, and central conflict. Users can prompt the agent for plot ideas if they encounter creative blocks. The slide notes that this conversational process, while taking a few minutes, is crucial for specifying all major elements of the movie, directly feeding into the hierarchical structure outlined in Slide 4. Ultimately, this agent creates a "premise.json" file, as shown in the workflow of Slide 5, setting the foundation for the subsequent stages of the agentic flow designed to overcome the long-form generation challenge from Slide 3. The A.W.E.S.O.M.-O. robot from Slide 1 is also visible in the top right corner.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that you've seen the whole flow, right? We're gonna kick things off with the 'Premise Agent.' This is our first, like, interactive chat agent, and it's where you actually discuss with it what your movie is *all* about. We're talking title, the logline – which is kinda like an executive summary, you know? – characters, genre, and the central conflict. And if you're stuck, dude, you can totally ask it to give you ideas for the plot! This chat might take a few minutes, even if you have a general idea, because it really makes you specify all the major elements. And then, boom, it creates our foundational `premise.json` file. Awesome.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Premise.json," visually presents the completed JSON file generated by the 'Premise Agent' described in the previous slide. It concretely shows the output of the initial step in the multi-stage screenplay generation process, as outlined in the "Screenplay Flow" of Slide 5. The JSON content includes a specific `project_id`, `title` ("Shadow of the Dark World"), and `writers`. Notably, the `format_notes` explicitly state that "Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat," directly linking this output to the AI character introduced in Slide 1 and the interactive process from Slide 6. Furthermore, it details a comprehensive `logline`, along with categorized `genre` (fantasy, action, adventure) and `themes` (hero's journey, destiny, etc.), encapsulating the core elements of the movie. This slide demonstrates how the agentic flow begins to break down the complex task of generating a full screenplay, addressing the 'one-shot' long-form challenge by establishing a solid, structured foundation.
slides.narration: Okay, so here it is, dude! This is our `premise.json` file, you know, the direct output from that Premise Agent we just talked about. See how it's all structured? Like, you've got the `project_id`, the `title`—'Shadow of the Dark World'—and, check it out, the `format_notes` even say 'Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat.' Pretty cool, right? We've also got the full `logline` here, and then, like, the `genre` and `themes` are all neatly categorized. This is, you know, the solid foundation for our movie, making sure we totally nail the core story before we move on.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Premise.json," continues to display the comprehensive JSON output generated by the 'Premise Agent,' building directly upon the partial view shown in the previous slide. It further details the core elements of the proposed movie screenplay, "Shadow of the Dark World." Specifically, the JSON code outlines the `central_conflict`, describing Link's heroic journey to prevent the sorcerer Aghanim from sacrificing Princess Zelda and unleashing Ganon upon the Light World. It also provides a detailed `setting`, including the `primary_time` as a "High fantasy era" and `primary_places` such as "Hyrule Castle" and "Dark World." Finally, it introduces the `protagonists`, specifically "Link," detailing his role as an "Unassuming hero" and his want to save his uncle and Zelda. This slide concretely demonstrates how the initial 'Premise' stage, part of the multi-stage 'Screenplay Flow' from Slide 5, effectively captures complex story components, reinforcing the agentic flow's approach to overcome the challenges of generating long-form documents.
slides.narration: Okay, so picking up right where we left off with this `premise.json`, you know, we're really diving into the guts of our story here. See that 'central_conflict' section? It lays out Link's whole race against time to stop Aghanim from sacrificing Zelda and, like, unleashing Ganon on the Light World. Pretty epic, right? And then, check it out, the 'setting' gives us that 'High fantasy era' vibe, with places like 'Hyrule Castle' and the 'Dark World.' And for 'protagonists,' dude, we've got Link, the 'unassuming hero,' whose big 'want' is to save his uncle and Zelda and, you know, prevent Hyrule from getting totally destroyed. This detailed JSON, it's just so critical for making sure we've got all the core pieces locked down before we even think about the next step.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Premise.json," continues and completes the comprehensive display of the JSON output generated by the 'Premise Agent,' building directly upon the partial views shown in the previous two slides. It finalizes the detailed entry for the `protagonist`, Link, specifying his role and ultimate goal to prevent destruction. Crucially, it introduces the `key_antagonist`, Aghanim, outlining his identity and objective to unleash Ganon. The slide also details a `key_ally`, Sahasrahla, and explicitly defines the overarching `stakes` of the story: the total conquest of the Light World. This complete `premise.json` file demonstrates how the initial step of the multi-stage 'Screenplay Flow' (Slide 5) meticulously establishes critical story components, directly supporting the agentic flow's approach to overcome the challenges of generating coherent long-form documents.
slides.narration: Okay, so, continuing with our `premise.json` here, right? We've finalized Link's whole deal – saving Hyrule from destruction, you know? But then, we introduce our `key_antagonist`: Aghanim, this evil sorcerer whose, like, whole want is to unleash Ganon on the Light World. Pretty intense, dude! And of course, every hero needs a `key_ally`, right? So we've got Sahasrahla, the wise village elder, who's gonna guide Link. And the `stakes`, man, they're huge: the total conquest of the Light World. This fully fleshed-out premise, it's just absolutely essential before we can even think about building out the whole story arc, you know?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Arc Agent," introduces the next stage in the multi-step screenplay generation process, directly following the 'Premise' stage detailed in the previous slides. It describes a chat agent designed to discuss and generate the movie's story arc, which consists of multiple acts. Crucially, this agent takes the `premise.json` file, created by the 'Premise Agent' (Slides 6-9), as its input for context, demonstrating the sequential nature of the 'Screenplay Flow' from Slide 5. Users interactively guide the agent, outlining the events for each act before prompting it to draft them one at a time. The ultimate output of this agent is an `arc.json` file, further building upon the structured approach to overcome the challenges of generating long-form documents, and the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O. robot from Slide 1 is visible in the top right corner.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've got our super solid `premise.json` all locked down, right? We're gonna move on to the next big step in our flow: the 'Arc Agent.' This, like, is still a chat agent, similar to the Premise Agent, but its whole job is to discuss and build out your movie's full story arc. And dude, the cool part is it takes that `premise.json` we just made as its input for all the context. So, you'll actually tell it what happens in each act, like, 'Act One is this, Act Two is that,' and then you prompt it to draft each act one at a time. This ensures we're building our story hierarchically, right? And then, boom, you get your `arc.json` file.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Arc.json," visually presents a portion of the completed JSON file generated by the 'Arc Agent' described in the previous slide, building upon the multi-stage screenplay generation process. It specifically displays details for `act: 3`, labeled as "Battle Aghanim and the Dark World," which is described as the climax and a major cliffhanger, estimated to be 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay. The JSON includes a `dramatic_question`, a comprehensive `summary` outlining Link's journey to confront Aghanim, and a `turning_point_end` where Aghanim banishes Link to the Dark World. It also lists several `must_hit_beats` detailing key plot points within this act. This slide concretely demonstrates how the 'Arc Agent' takes the detailed premise from previous slides and structures the narrative into acts, further breaking down the complex task of generating a full screenplay into manageable, coherent sections, thereby addressing the 'one-shot' long-form challenge.
slides.narration: Okay, so here it is, dude! This is our `arc.json` file, you know, the direct output from that Arc Agent we just talked about. See how it's all structured for, like, Act 3? It's labeled 'Battle Aghanim and the Dark World,' and it even has an `approx_pages_note` – pretty cool, right? You've got the `dramatic_question` and a full `summary` outlining Link's whole journey to confront Aghanim. And then, check out the `turning_point_end` where Aghanim banishes Link to the Dark World. This is just so crucial for laying out all those `must_hit_beats` and ensuring we're breaking down that complex story into totally coherent acts, you know, building on our premise.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Sequence Agent," introduces the next automated step in the multi-stage screenplay generation process, directly following the 'Arc Agent' detailed in the previous slides. Unlike the previous chat-based agents, this one is an automated agent tasked with writing the sequences for each act of the movie. It takes `premise.json` and `arc.json` as inputs, providing a global view of the movie, and also considers all previous sequences for local context, which helps combat issues like 'context drift' from Slide 3. The slide defines key sequence elements, including its narrative goal, key locations, and the 'exit beat' that transitions to the next sequence. Ultimately, this agent creates a `sequence.json` file, further detailing the screenplay's structure as part of the hierarchical 'Screenplay Flow' presented in Slide 5. The A.W.E.S.O.M.-O. robot from Slide 1 is also visible in the top right corner.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've got our `arc.json` all mapped out, right? We're moving on to the 'Sequence Agent.' And dude, this one's different—it's totally automated, no more chatting! Its whole job is to write the sequences for each act of your movie. And here's why it's so powerful: it takes both your `premise.json` *and* your `arc.json` for that big global view. But it also gets all the *previous sequences* generated so far, giving it critical local context. This dual context is, like, super key to battling that 'context drift' we talked about. It defines the goal for each sequence, the key locations, and even the 'exit beat' to transition. And then, boom, you get your `sequence.json` file.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Sequence.json," visually presents the output of the 'Sequence Agent' introduced in the previous slide, concretely detailing the first sequence generated for the "Shadow of the Dark World" screenplay. The JSON structure includes a `project_id`, `sequence_count`, and an array of `sequences`. It specifically highlights "SEQ1," titled "Zelda's Urgent Plea," which belongs to `act: 1`. This sequence outlines its `goal` to establish Link's call to adventure and equip him, sets an "Urgent, sorrowful, determined" `emotional_tone`, and lists `key_locations` such as "Link's Home" and "Hyrule Castle." Crucially, it defines the `exit_beat` as Link preparing to infiltrate Hyrule Castle. This automated step takes the overarching arc and premise from previous stages to create detailed, coherent narrative segments, further combating issues like 'context drift' mentioned in Slide 3. This output reinforces the multi-stage "agentic flow" (Slide 5) by breaking down the complex task of screenplay generation into well-defined, manageable sequences, directly addressing the "one-shot" long-form challenge.
slides.narration: Okay, so here it is, dude! This is our `sequence.json` file, the direct output from that automated Sequence Agent we just talked about. Check it out, we've got `SEQ1`, titled 'Zelda's Urgent Plea,' which is part of Act One. See the `goal`? It's all about establishing Link's call to adventure and equipping him with, like, his first tools for the journey. And the `emotional_tone` is 'Urgent, sorrowful, determined.' Then, the `key_locations` are Link's Home and Hyrule Castle. And that `exit_beat`? Link prepares to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle. This structured detail is so crucial, you know, for breaking down the arc into manageable, coherent chunks and totally preventing that context drift. Awesome!

slides.description: This slide, titled "Scene Agent," introduces the final, most granular automated step in the multi-stage screenplay generation process, directly building upon the 'Sequence Agent' from the previous slide. It describes an automated agent, without a chat interface, responsible for writing the individual scenes within each sequence of the movie. Crucially, this agent receives `premise.json`, `arc.json`, and `sequence.json` as inputs, providing a global understanding of the film, and also considers all previously generated scenes within the current and prior sequences for vital local context. This comprehensive input strategy is designed to combat issues like 'context drift' and maintain coherence, directly addressing the 'one-shot' long-form challenge from Slide 3. The slide defines primary scene elements, including the 'slug line' for location and 'blocks' for actions and dialogues, showcasing how the hierarchical structure of a screenplay (Slide 4) is fully realized. Ultimately, this agent creates a `scenes.json` file, completing the detailed breakdown of the 'Screenplay Flow' from Slide 5, and the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O. robot from Slide 1 is visible in the top right corner.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've got our sequences all lined up from that automated Sequence Agent, right? We're diving into the absolute, most granular level: the 'Scene Agent.' And dude, this is also totally automated, no more chatting! Its whole job is to take each sequence and write out all the individual scenes for the movie. Now, check out its power: it gets your `premise.json`, `arc.json`, *and* `sequence.json` for that full global picture. But, like, it *also* sees all the scenes already generated in the current and previous sequences. This combo of global and local context is, you know, super vital for battling context drift and making sure everything flows perfectly. It's gonna define the slug line, and then, like, all the blocks of action and dialogue. And then, boom, you get your `scenes.json` file. Pretty awesome, right?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Scenes.json," presents the concrete output generated by the 'Scene Agent' introduced in the previous slide, completing the multi-stage screenplay generation process. It displays the JSON structure for the initial scene, "S0001," which is part of "SEQ1" ("Zelda's Urgent Plea" from Slide 13). The scene details Link's home at night, his awakening to a telepathic plea from Princess Zelda (V.O.), and his immediate visions of Hyrule Castle and Aghanim. This granular output, broken down into action blocks and dialogue, demonstrates how the agentic flow (Slide 5) successfully translates the overarching story arc and sequence goals into individual, coherent scenes. By meticulously generating each scene, this step directly addresses the challenge of producing long-form documents without losing coherence, as highlighted in Slide 3.
slides.narration: Okay, so here it is, dude! This is our `scenes.json` file, you know, the direct output from that automated Scene Agent. And check it out, this is our very first scene, `S0001`, which is part of `SEQ1`, titled 'Zelda's Urgent Plea'. So, we've got Link waking up in his home, like, hearing Princess Zelda's desperate telepathic plea in his mind, right? And then, boom, he gets these visions of Hyrule Castle and Aghanim. This super granular output, broken down into action blocks and dialogue, it really shows how our whole agentic flow takes that big arc and those sequence goals and translates them into these individual, totally coherent scenes. Pretty awesome!

slides.description: This slide, titled "Enhancement Agents," introduces a concept for refining the outputs of the multi-stage screenplay generation process established in previous slides. It proposes that the 'agentic flow' can be improved by using dedicated agents to enhance the intermediate outputs, such as making sequences or scenes better, cleaning up the premise, or making the arc more creative. Visually, a golden robot labeled "ENHANCEMENT AGENT" is depicted alongside the text. A flowchart, building upon the 'Screenplay Flow' from Slide 5, illustrates this concept by placing small golden enhancement robots between the 'Premise', 'Arc', 'Sequences', and 'Scenes' stages, indicating that these agents process and refine the outputs (premise.json, arc.json, sequence.json, scenes.json) as they pass from one generation step to the next. This introduces a feedback loop to improve the quality of the long-form document, further addressing the challenges of coherence and context drift from Slide 3 by enhancing the work of the specific agents (Premise, Arc, Sequence, Scene Agents) introduced in Slides 6, 10, 12, and 14 respectively.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've got our whole screenplay generated, from premise all the way down to individual scenes, right? You might be thinking, 'Dude, what if I want to make that sequence even better?' Or 'What if I want to punch up the premise a little bit?' Well, this is where 'Enhancement Agents' come in! These are, like, dedicated agents that can go in and refine any of those intermediate outputs. So, yeah, they can help you write better sequences or scenes, clean up the premise, or even make your arc more creative, you know? It's like having little AI editors at every step, making sure everything is just top-notch.

slides.description: This slide, titled "Generalizing to Other Documents," concludes the discussion on the agentic flow by demonstrating its broader applicability, directly fulfilling the promise from Slide 2. It features a bullet point stating that by modifying agent prompts, the hierarchical flow developed for screenplays can be used for other long text documents. A prominent table illustrates this concept, mapping the "Premise," "Arc," "Sequence," and "Scene" stages—established as the core of the agentic flow in Slide 5 and detailed in subsequent slides—to equivalent structural elements across various document types like books, podcasts, songs, lectures, and business plans. For instance, a book's "Arc" is a "Table of contents" and its "Scene" is a "Sub-chapter," while a lecture's "Arc" is an "Outline" and its "Scene" is a "Slide." Each row in the table also includes an "Artist Rendering" showing the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O. robot from Slide 1 interacting with the respective document type, humorously reinforcing the AI's role. This slide effectively showcases how the multi-stage, hierarchical approach successfully addresses the 'one-shot' long-form generation challenge from Slide 3 by providing a structured framework for diverse content creation.
slides.narration: Okay, so now that we've seen the whole screenplay generated, and how to even enhance it, right? The *most* powerful part of this whole agentic flow, dude, is how incredibly generalizable it is. Like, you can take this *exact* hierarchical approach we used for screenplays—premise, arc, sequence, scene—and just by modifying the agent prompts, you can apply it to *any* long-form document. Check out this table: for a book, your 'arc' becomes the table of contents, and a 'scene' is a sub-chapter. For a lecture, like this one, your 'arc' is the outline, and each 'scene' is a slide. It's the same pattern, you know? Just different labels. Pretty wild, right?

slides.description: This slide, titled "Coding Session," serves as a practical call to action, building upon the theoretical framework of the agentic flow for long-form document generation. It presents three key objectives: writing a short screenplay using "Awesome-O," which directly leverages the multi-stage process detailed in Slides 5-15; adding enhancement agents to this flow, a concept introduced in Slide 16 to refine outputs; and modifying "Awesome-O" for different document types, expanding on the generalization promise from Slide 2 and exemplified in Slide 17. The slide prominently features a simplified flowchart, reminiscent of the "Screenplay Flow" from Slide 5, illustrating the sequential steps from "Premise" to "Arc," "Sequences," and "Scenes." Small A.W.E.S.O.M.-O. robots, first seen in Slide 1 and as agent representations, are depicted between each step, visually reinforcing the AI-driven, hierarchical generation process that combats the "one-shot" challenge from Slide 3.
slides.narration: Okay, so, like, we've covered a ton of ground, right? Now it's your turn to jump in! For our coding session, dude, here are some practical next steps. First, you totally gotta try writing a short screenplay using Awesome-O, putting that whole agentic flow to the test. Then, you can, like, totally add those enhancement agents we talked about to really punch up your content. And finally, the really cool part, modify Awesome-O to generate *different* document types, you know, seeing how generalizable this architecture really is. It's gonna be awesome to see what y'all build!
